figures.caption: Fig. 2: Discussion structure effects vary by model type. Creativity ratings across discussion methods. The dashed line and shaded region indicate the single-agent mean and 95% confidence interval (CI), respectively; error bars denote 95% CIs for multi-agent means. For GPT-4.1 (left), iterative refinement significantly outperforms the None (no discussion) condition (M = 0.308 vs. M = 0.212, β = +0.097, p < 0.001). Conversely, o3-high (right) maintains consistently high creativity across all structures (M = 0.322–0.345), with only instructed discussion showing a small taskadjusted improvement over None (β = +0.023, p = 0.047; other structured conditions p > 0.05).

Table C17: Hierarchical regression models predicting LLM creativity from trajectory features. Models 1-4: OLS regression with robust standard errors (HC3). Model 5: Mixed effects with task random intercepts (REML estimation). Outcome variable: standardized creativity rating (z-scored within LLM sample). All trajectory features standardized (z-scored). Conditional R² includes both fixed and random effects; Marginal R² includes fixed effects only. Reference categories: Task = education inequality, Model = Mixed, Discussion = none. ∗p < 0.10, ∗∗p < 0.05, ∗∗∗p < 0.01.

Table C16: Trajectory feature definitions. All features were computed from turnlevel embeddings using Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B. Here, dcos denotes cosine distance, ei is the embedding at turn i, ē is the conversation centroid, and n is the number of turns.

Table C23: Robustness check: Alternative embedding model (Qwen3-Embedding-4B vs Qwen3-Embedding0.6B). Standardized coefficients from OLS models predicting creativity from trajectory features with task fixed effects. Results are robust to embedding model choice, with consistent coefficient signs and significance patterns. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001.

Table C21: Manipulability of trajectory features through experimental design. Hierarchical OLS regression models with robust standard errors (HC3) showing variance explained (R²) in each trajectory feature by experimental design choices. Model 1: Task fixed effects only (baseline). Model 2: Task + Discussion Structure. Model 3: Task + Model Type. Model 4: Task + Discussion + Model (additive). Model 5: Task + Discussion × Model (with interaction). N=3,574 LLM conversations.

Table C22: Interaction effects in trajectory feature manipulability. Decomposition of variance explained into additive effects (discussion + model independently) and interaction effects (discussion × model). Features with high interaction percentages indicate that optimal discussion structures vary substantially by model type.

Table C15: Replacement pool effects on creativity. Matched conditions differing only in replacement pool availability (conditions 41–45 vs. 31–35). Values show mean (standard error). ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001.

Fig. 3: Trajectory analysis reveals mechanistic differences between LLM and human teams. (a) Standardized regression coefficients predicting creativity from trajectory features for LLM teams (blue) and human teams (purple). All features and outcomes are z-scored within sample. Both LLM and human teams produce more creative ideas when conversations range widely (negative global coherence). LLM teams additionally benefit from efficient exploration (positive semantic spread, negative path length and max distance). Human teams additionally benefit from smooth conversational flow (positive local coherence, positive trajectory curvature). For clarity here we only show the five features with the largest absolute LLM-human coefficient differences; all nine features are reported in Table C20. (b) Scaffolding benefit (open vs. iterative discussion) by model type. GPT-4.1 shows substantial creativity gains from iterative scaffolding (+37.4%), while o3-high shows minimal benefit (+4.0%), consistent with reduced dependence on external scaffolding in reasoning models.

Table C24: Side-by-side conversation comparison illustrating semantic exploration patterns. Each row shows the first sentence of each turn. Highcreativity conversation (left) exhibits dramatic conceptual leaps across semantic domains (global coherence = -0.91), while low-creativity conversation (right) iteratively refines a single core concept (global coherence = 2.20). Both conversations use the same discussion structures (instructed) and model (GPT-4.1) for the same task (plastic waste).

Table C19: Model-specific variance explained by trajectory features. Mixed effects models with task random intercepts. Trajectory features explain more variance for reasoning models (o3) than standard models (GPT-4.1).
Table C11: Top 5 highest-scoring ideas per task. Ideas are shown as harmonized paraphrases used for blinded evaluation. All top-scoring ideas were generated by LLM teams.

Table C25: Robustness check: Alternative creativity operationalization (Additive vs Multiplicative). Standardized coefficients from OLS models predicting creativity from trajectory features with task fixed effects. Multiplicative: Novelty × Usefulness. Additive: Novelty + Usefulness. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001.

Table 3: Experimental Task Prompts. The six tasks span environmental, logistical, hypothetical, social, organizational, and behavioral domains. All prompts concluded with the identical instruction: “You are collaborating with other team members to come up with one creative idea to [solve the problem]. The idea will be evaluated on its creativity (i.e., it should be both novel and useful).”

Table C12: Novelty-usefulness decomposition of discussion structure effects. Regression coefficients from parallel models predicting creativity (Model 4 from main analysis), novelty, and usefulness separately. Discussion structures that boost novelty systematically reduce usefulness, revealing a fundamental trade-off. GPT-4.1’s iterative discussion overcomes this trade-off by substantially increasing novelty (β = +0.208, p < 0.001) without proportionally reducing usefulness. Reference categories: discussion = none, model = Mixed. Standard errors in parentheses. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001.

Fig. 1: LLM teams substantially outperform human teams across multiple creativity dimensions. (A) Distribution of creativity, novelty, and usefulness ratings for human teams and multi-agent LLM teams. Box plots show median (line), interquartile range (box), and 1.5 times the interquartile range (whiskers); diamonds indicate means. LLM teams show large advantages in creativity (d = 1.50) and novelty (d = 1.29) while maintaining comparable usefulness (d = 0.08, p = 0.142). (B) Distribution of ideas in novelty-usefulness space. Each point represents one idea, colored by source (gray = human, red = LLM). Stars indicate group means with 95% confidence intervals. (C) Distribution of all ideas with 95th percentile thresholds marked (dashed lines). The LLM distribution is shifted rightward relative to the human distribution. (D) Box plots comparing the top 5% of ideas from each group (LLM: M = 0.53; Human: M = 0.37).

Table C18: Hierarchical regression models predicting human creativity from trajectory features. Models 1-2: OLS regression with robust standard errors (HC3). Model 3: Mixed effects with task random intercepts (REML estimation). Outcome variable: standardized creativity rating (zscored within human sample). All trajectory features standardized (z-scored). Conditional R² includes both fixed and random effects; Marginal R² includes fixed effects only. Reference category: Task = education inequality. ∗p < 0.10, ∗∗p < 0.05, ∗∗∗p < 0.01.
